sentences_input.text: Yeah, we were reunited in Auschwitz, and we-- from Auschwitz we went to Funfteichen.
And we were together on a death march, and-- and-- we must have lost maybe close to half the people that we started out of from Funfteichen to Gross-Rosen.
And Gross-Rosen, we were taken into barracks that didn't have any baths.
You-- there was no room because they-- they collected all these people to Gross-Rosen, the collector's camp.
And so we were-- we was sitting like a herring.
And you had to sit.
You-- you spread-- you had to spread your legs, and the next guy would sit in front of you.
People were sitting behind you, and if you had to go to the bathroom, the whole row would have to stand up because there was no room-- there were like wall-to-wall people.
And the-- and there were terrible conditions.
A lot of people got sick.
And there was diseases.
Well, we-- we were not-- we-- you did not-- you were not able to lie down to sleep.
You had to sit like packed in herring, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the facilities were limited.
There were not enough bathrooms and not enough-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a shortage of everything because they-- they bunched in so many people to this camp.
So we-- but one of the group, and one of the brothers from-- from the butchers, worked in the kitchen.
They got to-- they were assigned to the kitchen and cooks, and they were able to bring some food.
And that was helping out the rest of it.
But we had to be very careful because people, if they knew that you had food on you, the Russians or the gypsies, they would stab you at night and take it away from you.
So-- so I used to walk around and-- we never worked in-- in this camp-- this was only a camp to be redistributed or to be whatever they-- they were planning to do.
The-- How long were you in Gross-Rosen?
Not too long, not too long at all, a few weeks.
From Gross-Rosen, we-- we were sent on an open rail, you know, rail-- railroad cars-- cattle-- real cattle cars and no roof.

And-- and we-- we was sent to a few camps, I think, where-- and they used to pull us over on the side, and we used to stand there for a day or two because their wehrmacht had the first priority to-- to go through.
We were-- we were sent somewheres to a camp where they didn't have the room for us, and we were parked outside of Dresden.
And it was raining, it was terrible weather, and cold, and we were parked-- we was-- put there-- and we stood there for quite a while.

Maybe like 100 yards away from us, there was another train with American prisoners, which they were-- they at least had food, Red Cross, and-- and they were treated more human like, at least, till that point.
But they were bombing Dresden at that time, and there was a direct hit on that transport of American prisoners.
And many people were killed, and some of them that survived tried to run away so they could save their life.
And-- and we-- the wehrmacht-- I think it was wehrmacht-- they set up machine guns under the train and mowed them all down.
It was a horrible sight to watch because the Americans were our final solution to stay alive, so we were-- that was a sad moment for the whole transport.
Then we finally-- they shuffled us around, and we finally arrived at this camp called Hersbruck.
That's not far from Nuremberg.

In Hersbruck, they were building-- we were building tunnels, big tunnels, part of them were already tiled, you know, like a-- what-- what is to be a underground factory for the Messerschmitt jet plane, because everything was-- they bombed everything else, so they-- that was what they wanted to do.
So we were-- that was a terrible camp, that Hersbruck, because we walked for quite a while-- at least one hour or two to the camp to the work site.
Walked, right.
And the same thing in the evening.
And then when we come home in the evening, we had to clean up the camp, police the area, and-- and received very little sleep there.
So people working like zombies and resigned to the fact that they're going-- not going to make-- make it.
This was a terrible sight because every time we walked, and people would run-- start running so they can be shot, you know, so-- because they knew they will be shot.
